preFix.title: Re-webbing a drop-in chair seat
preFix.subtitle: Replace sagging jute or rubber webbing on a drop-in upholstery seat frame
preFix.excerpt: How to strip the old webbing from a drop-in chair seat frame and fit new jute or rubber webbing in an interlaced grid pattern, stretching each strip taut before stapling to give a firm base for the seat pad.
preFix.sourceNotes: The Practical Upholsterer (1917). Cassell's Household Guide (upholstery). Dix and Miller Modern Upholstery (1948).

content.text: with sagging webbing gives a chair a tired, sunken look and provides poor support. Replacing the webbing with new jute strips in an
restores the seat to a firm, flat base. The webbing does all the load-carrying work: if it is not stretched taut before stapling, it will sag again within months.

Jute webbing: 50 mm black-and-white jute is the standard for drop-in seats. Measure the seat frame opening (front to back and side to side) and calculate the number of strips needed at 50 mm wide with 10 mm gaps between strips. Allow 150 mm extra per strip for turning back on itself at the staple. T50 staples in 10 mm length for the webbing layer. For a modern seat frame, rubber Pirelli webbing (which needs no interlacing) is an alternative for front-to-back strips only.
Step 1, remove the old webbing and clean the frame

Flip the seat frame upside down. Use the staple remover to lift each staple from the frame, then pull the old webbing free. If old tacks were used instead of staples, lever them out with the staple remover or a small screwdriver. Remove all old staples and tacks: a new staple driven into a hole left by an old one has no grip. Clear any splinters from the frame top edges with the tack hammer.

Work with the frame flat on the bench, upside down. Fold the end of the first jute strip back 25 mm on itself. Position it at one end of the frame rail and drive three 10 mm staples through the doubled fold into the rail, in a triangle pattern. Pull the strip across to the opposite rail with the webbing stretcher: hook the teeth of the stretcher into the webbing and push the stretcher handle downward over the rail edge. The webbing should feel drum-tight. Without releasing the stretcher, drive three staples through the webbing into the rail, then cut the webbing 25 mm past the staple line and fold the tail back over the staples.
Step 3, weave and attach the side-to-side strips

Fit the side-to-side strips by weaving them through the front-to-back strips before stapling. Hold the new strip at one end rail and weave it alternately over and under each front-to-back strip. Staple the woven end to the rail, then pull the free end through with the webbing stretcher and staple at the other rail. Each strip should alternate its over-under sequence from the strip below it, giving the interlaced pattern.

Rubber Pirelli webbing is fitted front to back only, without interlacing: it stretches under load and provides its own lateral stability. Fix each end with three staples through the supplied metal clip. The clip distributes the staple load across the full width of the webbing end. Pirelli webbing does not need a webbing stretcher: clip one end, stretch to the other rail by hand, and clip the second end.

Jute webbing that is correctly tensioned and not overloaded lasts 20 to 30 years before needing replacement. A seat that is regularly overloaded (by standing on it) stretches the webbing faster. If one strip breaks, replace all the strips in that direction at the same time: a single new strip in a grid of old strips will be tighter than the rest and will carry a disproportionate share of the load.

attrs.fix: Remove and re-fit the strips using the webbing stretcher. The strip must feel drum-tight when the stretcher is in position. If the frame is narrow, improvise the stretch by levering the strip over the rail edge with a length of batten.
attrs.cause: The strips were not tensioned tightly enough before stapling; hand tension alone is not sufficient for jute webbing.
attrs.symptom: Webbing strips sag within weeks of fitting

attrs.fix: Move each staple position 5 to 8 mm away from the old hole. For a badly split rail, reinforce with a strip of 6 mm ply glued and clamped across the face before re-stapling.
attrs.cause: The rail timber has split along the grain from previous staple holes; or the timber is too soft for 10 mm staples.
attrs.symptom: Staples pull out of the rail within weeks
